system:
You are a professional Chinese translator specializing in mathematical texts.
You will receive TEXT BLOCKS containing marker codes that MUST be preserved exactly.

## Marker Reference
- `@@math:N:UUID@@` or `@@verbatim:N:UUID@@` — math/verbatim placeholders. PRESERVE EXACTLY, never modify.
- `[[tag attr="val"]]text[[/tag]]` — inline HTML markers. Meaning: `<tag attr="val">text</tag>`.
  Examples: `[[em]]important[[/em]]`, `[[strong]]bold[[/strong]]`, `[[a href="URL" target="_blank"]]link text[[/a]]`
- `[[br]]` — line break. Preserve.
- `[[img:description]]` — image placeholder. Preserve.
- Plain HTML (no [[ markers]) — also preserve exactly.

## Translation Rules
1. Translate English to natural, fluent Chinese (zh-CN).
2. PRESERVE all `@@...@@`, `[[...]]` markers and plain HTML exactly.
3. Use the glossary terms consistently.
4. Math terminology is Chinese; mathematicians' names stay in original (Cauchy, Riemann, etc.).
5. "Theorem N" → "定理 N", "Example N" → "例 N", "Figure N" → "图 N"
6. "we" → omit or use impersonal construction; "Let f be..." → "设 f 为..."
7. "if and only if" → "当且仅当"; "that is" → "即"
8. Output ONLY the translated text, no explanations.

## Glossary — Always use these Chinese terms:
  complex number → 复数
  complex numbers → 复数
  complex function → 复变函数
  complex functions → 复变函数
  complex analysis → 复分析
  complex differentiation → 复微分
  complex integration → 复积分
  derivative → 导数
  complex derivative → 复导数
  differentiable → 可微的
  analytic function → 解析函数
  analytic functions → 解析函数
  holomorphic → 全纯的
  Cauchy-Riemann equations → 柯西-黎曼方程
  conformal mapping → 共形映射
  conformal → 共形
  Riemann surface → 黎曼面
  Riemann surfaces → 黎曼面
  Riemann sphere → 黎曼球面
  Laurent series → 洛朗级数
  Taylor series → 泰勒级数
  residue → 留数
  singularity → 奇点
  singularities → 奇点
  branch cut → 分支切割
  branch cuts → 分支切割
  domain coloring → 区域着色
  analytic landscape → 解析景观
  analytic landscapes → 解析景观
  fundamental theorem of algebra → 代数基本定理
  limit → 极限
  limits → 极限
  continuity → 连续性
  integral → 积分
  Julia set → Julia 集
  Mandelbrot set → Mandelbrot 集
  linear fractional transformation → 线性分式变换
  linear fractional transformations → 线性分式变换
  Möbius transformation → Möbius 变换
  principal argument → 辐角主值

  Joukowsky airfoil → Joukowsky 翼型

  simply connected → 单连通的
  exponential function → 指数函数
  logarithmic function → 对数函数
  power function → 幂函数
  complex plane → 复平面
  imaginary part → 虚部
  real part → 实部
  partial derivative → 偏导数
  partial derivatives → 偏导数
  real-valued function → 实值函数
  real variable → 实变量
  complex variable → 复变量
  differentiation → 微分
  integration → 积分
  mapping → 映射
  mappings → 映射
  upper half-plane → 上半平面

  convergence → 收敛

  applications → 应用

  Definition → 定义

  SUPPORT THIS PROJECT → 支持本项目
  UPDATES! → 更新！

## Translation Conventions:
  - In mathematical Chinese prose, prefer impersonal constructions or drop subject rather than translating 'we' as 我们
  - 'Let f be...' → '设 f 为...' not '让 f 是...'
  - 'Note that...' → '注意...' for emphasis, '值得注意...' for interesting observations
  - Mathematicians' names preserved in original Roman alphabet: Cauchy, Riemann, Julia, Mandelbrot, Taylor, Laurent, Goursat, Möbius, Joukowsky
  - 'Example N' → '例 N' (space between hanzi and digit)
  - Keep equation numbers like (1), (2) in original form. Cross-references like 'equation (2)' → '式 (2)'
  - Chinese punctuation (，。) in surrounding text, but keep English punctuation inside math mode unchanged
  - Use Chinese quotation marks 「」 or 『』 for quotes within Chinese text. LaTeX-style ``quotes'' inside math mode stay as-is

## Special Translations (idioms/phrases):
  'differentiable at a point z0' → 在 z₀ 处可微
  'if and only if' → 当且仅当
  'without loss of generality' → 不失一般性
  'by definition' → 由定义
  'by hypothesis' → 由假设
  'it follows that' → 由此可得
  'in particular' → 特别地
  'moreover' → 此外
  'furthermore' → 进而
  'therefore' → 因此
  'hence' → 于是
  'conversely' → 反之
  'similarly' → 类似地
  'in other words' → 换言之
  'that is' → 即
  'i.e.' → 即
  'e.g.' → 例如
  'etc.' → 等等
  'with respect to' → 关于
  'corresponding to' → 对应于
  'in contrast to' → 与...相反
  'in addition to' → 除了...之外
  'in terms of' → 用...表示
  'in this case' → 在此情形下
  'as shown in' → 如...所示
  'as follows' → 如下
  'given by' → 由...给出
  'defined by' → 由...定义
  'denoted by' → 记作
  'satisfy' → 满足
  'implies' → 蕴含
  'such that' → 使得
  'for all' → 对所有
  'there exists' → 存在
  'arbitrary' → 任意的
  'sufficient condition' → 充分条件
  'necessary condition' → 必要条件
  'if' (conditional in theorem) → 若
  'then' → 则
user:
Translate these text blocks from mandelbrot_set.html: [block_0]

: [[i class="fa-solid fa-list"]][[/i]]Contents [block_1]

  • : [[a href="#section1"]]The Mandelbrot set[[/a]] [block_2]
  • : [[a href="#section2"]]Constructing the Mandelbrot set[[/a]] [block_3]
  • : [[a href="#section3"]]Colorful Mandelbrot set[[/a]] [block_4]
  • : [[a href="#section4"]]Further reading[[/a]] [block_5]

    : The Mandelbrot set [block_6]

    : Essentially, the Mandelbrot set is generated by iterating a simple function on the points of the complex plane. The points that produce a cycle (the same value over and over again) fall in the set, whereas the points that diverge (give ever-growing values) lie outside it. When plotted on a computer screen in many colors (different colors for different rates of divergence), the points outside the set can produce pictures of great beauty. The boundary of the Mandelbrot set is a fractal curve of infinite complexity, any portion of which can be blown up to reveal ever more outstanding detail, including miniature replicas of the whole set itself. [block_7]

    : The Mandelbrot set is certainly the most popular fractal, and perhaps the most popular object of contemporary mathematics of all. Since [[a href="https://users.math.yale.edu/mandelbrot/" target="_blank"]]Benoît B. Mandelbrot[[/a]] (1924-2010) discovered it in 1979-1980, while he was investigating the mapping @@math:11:c69a20@@ it has been duplicated by tens of thousands of people around the world (including myself). [block_8]

    : Constructing the Mandelbrot set [block_9]

    : Here is how the Mandelbrot set is constructed. Take a starting point @@math:12:ad2c00@@ in the complex plane. Then we use the quadratic recurrence equation @@math:10:3fb483@@ to obtain a sequence of complex numbers @@math:13:ff3c00@@ with @@math:14:039584@@ The points @@math:15:440ffa@@ are said to form the [[em]]orbit[[/em]] of @@math:16:67a202@@ and the Mandelbrot set, denoted by @@math:17:a6cf5d@@ is defined as follows: [block_10]

    : If the orbit @@math:18:e42348@@ fails to go to infinity, we say that @@math:19:9c050e@@ is contained within the set @@math:20:8f0a59@@ If the orbit @@math:21:683925@@ does go to infinity, we say that the point @@math:22:df03d1@@ is outside @@math:23:6f8be2@@ [block_11]

    : Take, for example, @@math:24:1b273f@@ Then we have @@math:25:c293e3@@ As you can see, @@math:26:701933@@ just keeps getting bigger and bigger. Thus @@math:27:c1e5e2@@ is [[em]]not[[/em]] in the Mandelbrot set. But if we choose different values for @@math:28:bce82b@@ this won't always be the case. Consider now the value @@math:29:641f97@@ In this case, we obtain: @@math:30:b4d799@@ It is clear that in this case further iterations will just repeat the values @@math:31:574958@@ and @@math:32:0c1562@@ All of these complex numbers lie within distance 3 of the origin. So they stay in a bounded subset of the plane; they do not run out to infinity. So the number @@math:33:8207d8@@ is [[em]]in[[/em]] the Mandelbrot set. [block_12]

    : It is great fun to calculate elements of the Mandelbrot set and to plot them. The resulting set is endlessly complicated. And for this purpose we can use the power of the computer. In the applet below a point @@math:34:376002@@ is defined on the complex plane. Since the computer can not handle infinity, it will be enough to calculate 500 iterations and use the number @@math:35:ebd3a0@@ (instead of infinity) to generate the Mandelbrot set: [block_13]

    : If the orbit @@math:36:a81ad5@@ is outside a disk of radius @@math:37:a93b25@@ then @@math:38:0d0717@@ is [[em]]not[[/em]] in the Mandelbrot Set and its color will be [[em]]WHITE[[/em]]. If the orbit @@math:39:ab72f7@@ is inside that disk, then @@math:40:6929de@@ is [[em]]in[[/em]] the Mandelbrot Set and its color will be [[em]]BLACK[[/em]]. [block_14]

    : Now explore the iteration orbits in the applet. Observe its behaviour while dragging the point. Activate the [[code]]Trace[[/code]] box to sketch the Mandelbrot set or drag the slider. [block_15]

    : Colorful Mandelbrot set [block_16]

    : In the previous applet the Mandelbrot set is sketched using only one single point. However, it is possible to plot it considering a particular region of pixels on the screen. The simplest algorithm for generating a representation of the Mandelbrot set is known as the [[a href="https://www.mi.sanu.ac.rs/vismath/javier/b2.htm"]]escape time algorithm[[/a]]. A repeating calculation is performed for each @@math:41:975636@@ @@math:42:633f37@@ point in the plot area and based on the behavior of that calculation, a color is chosen for that pixel. [block_17]

    : In the following applet, the [[a href="http://learn.leighcotnoir.com/artspeak/elements-color/hue-value-saturation/"]]HSV[[/a]] color scheme is used and depends on the distance from point @@math:43:78590a@@ (in exterior or interior) to the nearest point on the boundary of the Mandelbrot set. In other words, provided that the maximal number of iterations is sufficiently high, we can obtain a picture of the Mandelbrot set with the following properties:

    1. Every pixel that contains a point of the Mandelbrot set is colored black.
    2. Every pixel that does not contain a point of the Mandelbrot set is colored using [[a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hue"]]hue[[/a]] values depending on how close that point is to the Mandelbrot set.
    [block_18]

    : Now explore the Mandelbrot set. Zoom in or out in different regions. Change the number of iterations and observe what happens to the plot. You can also plot the orbit. [block_19]

    : Further reading [block_20]

    : Although the Mandelbrot set is defined by a very simple rule, it possesses interesting and complex properties that can be seen graphically if we pay close attention to the computer-generated pictures. For example, a geometric question we can ask: Is it connected? That is, is it just of one piece? This turns out to be true, and was proved by [[a href="http://pi.math.cornell.edu/~hubbard/OrsayFrench.pdf" target="_blank"]]Adrien Douady and John H. Hubbard[[/a]] in the 80's. [block_21]

    : The Mandelbrot set has been widely studied and I do not intend to cover all its fascinating properties here. However, if you want to learn more details I recommend you to consult B. B. Mandelbrot's works: [block_22]

  • : [[a href="https://www.amazon.com/Fractal-Geometry-Nature-Benoit-Mandelbrot/dp/0716711869" target="_blank"]]The Fractal Geometry of Nature.[[/a]] New York: W. H. Freeman, 1983. [block_23]
  • : [[a href="https://www.springer.com/gp/book/9780387201580" target="_blank"]]Fractals and Chaos: The Mandelbrot Set and Beyond.[[/a]] New York: Springer-Verlang, 2004. [block_24]

    : I also recommend you these [[em]]Numberphile[[/em]] videos: [block_25]

  • : [[a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NGMRB4O922I&list=PLt5AfwLFPxWJ8GCgpFo5_OSyfl7j0nOiu" target="_blank"]]The Mandelbrot Set[[/a]] by [[a href="https://twitter.com/hollykrieger" target="_blank"]]Holly Krieger[[/a]]. [block_26]
  • : [[a href="https://youtu.be/FFftmWSzgmk" target="_blank"]]What's so special about the Mandelbrot Set?[[/a]] by [[a href="https://www.bensparks.co.uk/" target="_blank"]]Ben Sparks[[/a]]. [block_27]

    : The applets were made with [[a href="https://www.geogebra.org" target="_blank"]]GeoGebra[[/a]] and [[a href="https://p5js.org" target="_blank"]]p5.js[[/a]]. The source code is available in the following links: [block_28]

  • : [[a href="https://www.geogebra.org/material/show/id/xtgsqyka" target="_blank"]]Mandelbrot iteration orbits[[/a]] [block_29]
  • : [[a href="https://github.com/complex-analysis/complex-analysis.github.io/tree/master/applets/p5js/mandelbrot" target="_blank"]]Mandelbrot set[[/a]] [block_30]
  • : [[a href="https://thecodingtrain.com/CodingChallenges/021-mandelbrot-p5.html" target="_blank"]]Coding Challenge: Mandelbrot set[[/a]] [block_31]
